Interpretation of Materiality 
The general standard set forth in Regulation 18702, according to 
Chainnan Lowenstein I is to be interpreted in the follcwing fashion: 
(1) 
If the facts suggest that the official "WOuld be biased in his 
decision-making, the decision \vill have a material effect. 
(2) 
If the quantitative guidelines are met, the decision

does not necessarily mean that materiality exists since quantitative guide-
lines are only guidelines and not absolute standards. 
(3) 
If a decision clearly will have a material effect on an official's 
financial interests, the official may not avoid disqualification by offering 
evidence that he is not biased. For example -- assume that a decision will 
cause an investment to drop py 30 percent and $100,000. 
By any measurerrent,

sections.question_synthetic: How should the materiality standard in Regulation 18702 be interpreted when determining whether an official must be disqualified from decision-making due to potential conflicts of interest?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Materiality exists when: (1) facts suggest bias in decision-making; (2) quantitative guidelines are met; or (3) a decision will clearly have a material effect on financial interests, even if the official claims lack of bias. The analysis prioritizes objective financial impact over subjective assessments of bias when facts clearly demonstrate material effect.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Draft memo to file with no formal Q/A structure. Document appears incomplete (text cuts off mid-sentence). Interpreted as internal FPPC correspondence rather than formal advice letter due to 'MEMO TO FILE' header and lack of addressee/formal response structure. Materiality interpretation guidance extracted from body text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should the materiality standard in Regulation 18702 be interpreted when determining whether an official must be disqualified from decision-making due to potential conflicts of interest?

CONCLUSION: Materiality exists when: (1) facts suggest bias in decision-making; (2) quantitative guidelines are met; or (3) a decision will clearly have a material effect on financial interests, even if the official claims lack of bias. The analysis prioritizes objective financial impact over subjective assessments of bias when facts clearly demonstrate material effect.

embedding.summary: This is a memorandum from Ted Prim interpreting the materiality standard under Regulation 18702, establishing a framework for when officials must be disqualified based on bias, quantitative guidelines, or clear material financial impact.
